turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Thomas has the opinions of the Court in the next two cases.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: The first opinion I have to announce is EPA v. Calumet Shreveport Refining. This case comes to us on a writ of certiorari to the Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit. The venue provision of the Clean Air Act establishes a three-part framework for determining where Clean Air Act case's litigation should be filed. Challenges to nationally applicable EPA actions belong in the D.C. Circuit, while challenges to locally or regionally applicable EPA actions presumptively belong in a regional circuit, but the provision makes an exception for locally or regionally applicable actions that are "based on a determination of nationwide scopeor effect", if EPA in taking that action made a finding of that basis. Those actions also can be challenged only in the D.C. Circuit. This case requires us to decide which venue category covers EPA's denials of small refinery exemption petitions under the Renewable Fuel Program. In its notices resolving the petitions, EPA stated that any challenges should be filed in the D.C. Circuit, either because those notices were nationally applicable actions oralternativelynationwide scope or effect, or that the nationwide scope or effect exception applied. Nevertheless, a group of small refineries challenged EPA's denials in the Fifth Circuitand that court denied EPA's motion to transfer. In an opinion filed with the clerk today, we vacate the Fifth Circuit's judgment. We agree with the Fifth Circuit that EPA's actions were locally or regionally applicable, but we conclude that the nationwide scope or effect exception applies. The parties do not dispute that EPA found that this exception applies, and two determinations of nationwide scope or effect form the primary driver of EPA's decision to deny the petitions. Its interpretation of the statutory standard for small refinery exemption requests and its nationwide economic analysis of how small refineries recover costs because the nationwide scope or effect exception applies, the Fifth Circuit should have transferred this case to the D.C. Circuit. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we vacate the judgment of the Fifth Circuit and remand for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Justice Gorsuch has filed a dissenting opinion in which the Chief Justice joins.
